runs.response:
Snipara tech stack:

- Frontend: Next.js 14, Tailwind CSS, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Backend (web API): Next.js API Routes, tRPC  
- MCP Server: Python FastAPI  
- Database: PostgreSQL with Prisma  
- Auth: NextAuth.js  
- Billing: Stripe  
- Cache / Rate limiting: Redis  
- Deployment: Vercel (web) and Railway (MCP)

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it drastically reduces large documentation into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction — e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens) that fits a client’s token budget so the client’s own LLM can produce accurate responses. Key benefits: LLM‑agnostic (no vendor lock‑in), no LLM inference costs passed through, precise token budgeting, and higher-margin, compute‑only delivery.

Noted total: Snipara exposes 39 MCP tools (25 Context tools + 14 Agent tools).

Tools explicitly named in the docs you provided:

- Core / primary
  - rlm_context_query (primary context tool)

- Memory tools
  - rlm_remember
  - rlm_recall
  - rlm_memories
  - rlm_forget

- Shared context / templates
  - rlm_shared_context
  - rlm_list_templates
  - rlm_get_template
  - rlm_list_collections (listed as a partial tool)

- Upload / document
  - rlm_upload_shared_document (partial; input schema shown)

- REPL / MCP-as-server tools
  - execute_python
  - get_repl_context
  - set_repl_context
  - clear_repl_context

- Partial tools (have client methods but no VS Code command)
  - rlm_stats
  - rlm_read
  - rlm_list_collections
  - rlm_upload_shared_document
  - rlm_settings

- Missing (no client method / no command)
  - rlm_request_access

Notes:
- The docs reference many more context tools (25 total) and multi-agent / swarm tools (10 tools) but do not enumerate every name in the excerpt you provided.
- For the complete, canonical list see the full reference at /docs/mcp-tools (per the article).

Snipara’s documented pricing tiers (by plan name) are:

- FREE — $0 (Context plan: 100 queries/month)
- PRO — $19/month (Context plan: 5,000 queries/month)
- TEAM — $49/month (Context plan: 20,000 queries/month)
- ENTERPRISE — $499/month (Context plan: Unlimited)

Additional product pricing entries in the docs show Context subscription product IDs with different monthly prices (e.g., PRO $29, TEAM $79, ENTERPRISE = Custom) and separate Agents subscriptions (STARTER $9, PRO $29, TEAM $79). The documentation contains multiple price listings, so you may want to confirm which table is authoritative for billing.
Not found in documentation

Short summary — Snipara manages token budgets by counting real tokens (with tiktoken), prioritizing and selecting document sections greedily by relevance until a token budget is filled, and performing smart truncation when a partial section must be used.

Key points

- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to measure tokens precisely rather than characters/words.
  - All selection decisions are based on token counts.

- Search & ranking
  - Supports search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (fuse keyword + semantic results).
  - Candidate document sections are scored/re-ranked by relevance before selection.

- Greedy selection within budget
  - Sections are considered highest-relevance first.
  - Each section’s token count is checked; sections are added while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
  - Stops when adding another full section would exceed max_tokens.

- Partial sections and smart truncation
  - If a section does not fully fit but there is remaining budget, Snipara uses smart_truncate to cut the content to the remaining tokens.
  - smart_truncate truncates at sentence boundaries (. ! ?) (no mid-sentence cuts), appends "..." and marks the section as truncated.
  - The response sets a truncated flag at section and overall response level when appropriate.

- Context composition & metadata
  - Returned context includes detailed metadata per section: file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag.
  - Response also includes total_tokens used, session_context (if any), and suggestions.
  - System instructions may be auto-injected and session context can be included (session context limited to a portion of budget — implementation notes mention up to ~20% of budget).

- Budget allocation strategies (higher-level)
  - There are default/category allocation profiles shown in the code: examples include CATEGORY_BUDGETS (e.g., MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE with priority weights) and a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION used by the web service that prioritizes project docs and shared categories (project ≈ 40% in examples).
  - The front-end/service logic can allocate per-category budgets and then select within each allocation (allocateWithinBudget sorts by relevance and fills category sub-budgets).

- Hard limits & policies
  - There are enforced limits (examples in code) such as max tokens per collection, max docs per collection, and minimum relevance thresholds that help keep budgets bounded.

- Multi-query & orchestration
  - Higher-level tools (multi_query, decompose) distribute budgets across multiple sub-queries and call the context query tool with per-query budgets.

- Outputs & observability
  - The context-query tool returns structured results (sections list, token totals, truncated flag, suggestions) and usage metrics (search_time_ms, sections_scanned).
  - Token-efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) are computed without calling an LLM.

Practical flow (example)
- Client calls rlm_context_query with query, max_tokens, search_mode.
- Engine searches (keyword/semantic/hybrid), ranks sections, and iterates in relevance order:
  - Adds full sections while under budget.
  - If a section would overflow, uses smart_truncate to fit remaining tokens (cut at sentence boundary), marks it truncated, and ends selection.
- Returns selected sections with metadata and total token usage.

If you want, I can walk through a concrete example (given a small set of sections and a max_tokens value) to show exactly which sections would be selected and where truncation would occur.

Shared context collections are team- or organization-level bundles of reusable documentation (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, templates, examples) that you upload once and then automatically include in every linked project so the team’s AI-driven tooling follows the same rules.

How they work (high level)
- Create a collection (Team admin) and upload documents (e.g., coding standards, architecture rules).
- Link that collection to one or more projects via the dashboard (Project > Shared Contexts).
- When a project runs queries, the platform automatically merges that collection’s documents into the project’s query context so model responses follow team rules.
- Projects inherit shared docs automatically; project-specific docs take precedence over shared items.

Key concepts and behavior
- Document categories (priority order):  
  - MANDATORY (highest) — non‑negotiable rules (40% token budget)  
  - BEST_PRACTICES (high) — recommended patterns (30% budget)  
  - GUIDELINES (medium) — helpful suggestions (20% budget)  
  - REFERENCE (low) — background/examples (10% budget)
- Within a category you can set document priority (0–100) to control importance.
- Token budget allocation: returned shared context is split by category percentages above, and rlm_shared_context can merge multiple linked collections accordingly.
- Project-level ordering: projects can reorder linked collections (lower items = higher priority in the UI note), and conflicts are surfaced when linking collections.

Common APIs / tools (examples from docs)
- rlm_shared_context(...) — fetch merged shared context (filterable by categories, max_tokens, include_project_docs).
- rlm_context_query(...) — query project context; shared context is automatically included in results.
- rlm_inject(...) — inject shared context into the session so it applies to subsequent queries.
- rlm_list_templates(...) and rlm_get_template(...) — list and retrieve prompt templates stored in collections.
- rlm_multi_project_query(...) — search across multiple team projects (team feature).

Typical workflow
1. Team admin: create collection, upload MANDATORY + BEST_PRACTICES documents.  
2. Link collection to projects (dashboard). Projects now inherit the docs.  
3. Developers call rlm_shared_context at session start or let rlm_context_query include it automatically.  
4. Implement code with the injected standards (examples show enforcing Zod validation, Prisma usage, etc.).  
5. Record decisions (rlm_remember) to keep a trace of standards-driven implementation.

Plan requirement and access
- Shared context collections and related team features require a Team (or Enterprise) plan; not available on Free/Pro.

If you want, I can summarize the exact API parameters to call for rlm_shared_context or give a short example of injecting team standards into a session.

Short summary — Snipara uses the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). Key points:

- User experience
  - The user runs a CLI command, is shown a URL and a short code, visits the URL in a browser, enters the code and authenticates in the browser.

- Token polling
  - The CLI (device) polls the MCP token endpoint to exchange the device_code for an access token:
    - Endpoint example: POST /api/oauth/device/token
    - Request body example:
      {
        "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
        "device_code": "abc123...",
        "client_id": "snipara_cli"
      }
    - Pending response: { "error": "authorization_pending" }
    - Success response: { "access_token":"snipara_at_...", "token_type":"Bearer", "expires_in":2592000, "refresh_token":"snipara_rt_..." }

- Tokens and validation
  - Access tokens use the prefix snipara_at_.
  - Tokens are validated by a DB lookup and expiry check (validate_oauth_token()) and scopes are enforced.

- Where tokens are stored and authentication priority
  - The client checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json for a stored OAuth token first; if absent/fails it falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key).
  - Invalid/expired tokens return HTTP 401 with a message prompting re-authentication (or running /snipara:quickstart).

- Workarounds / alternatives
  - To force API-key auth instead of OAuth, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client env (or delete/move ~/.snipara/tokens.json).

If you want the exact request/response examples or the endpoint path again, tell me which part to expand.

The main database models are:
- User
- Project
- TeamMember

(Prisma models are PascalCase singular; their tables are mapped to snake_case plural, e.g., users, projects, team_members.)

Snipara uses a strict layered/import-hierarchy architecture:

- Routes (app/api/) → Services (lib/services/) → Repositories (lib/db/queries/) → Utils & Types (no dependencies)  
  (Lower layers must not import from higher layers.)

Key points:
- Repository pattern: all DB access goes through lib/db/queries/ (no direct prisma in routes).  
- Thin route handlers: authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to service/query, return response.  
- Services handle external APIs (lazy init, retries).

Short answer
- Snipara will search, rank and assemble only the most relevant pieces of your 500K‑token codebase into a compact context that fits your model’s window. The docs give an example reduction from 500K → ~5K tokens, so you feed the LLM a tiny, highly relevant context instead of the whole repo.
- That example implies a token reduction of (500,000 − 5,000) / 500,000 = 99% (documentation also states a “90% Context Reduction” as a general claim — actual reduction depends on your data and query).
- Exact dollar savings depend on the LLM’s token pricing (not provided in the docs), but the percentage reduction in tokens translates directly to the same percentage reduction in token-based inference cost.

How Snipara achieves this (from the docs)
- Token budgeting: you pass max_tokens (e.g., <= your 128K window) and Snipara greedily picks highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is filled.
- Accurate token counting with tiktoken (cl100k_base) so selections match the real model token cost.
- Smart truncation: if a section only partially fits, it is truncated at a sentence boundary and marked truncated.
- Metadata & session context: returned sections include file/line, relevance scores, token counts; session context can be included (up to ~20% of the budget) and system instructions can be auto‑injected.
- Result: an optimized context payload that fits your model window and contains only the most relevant content.

Estimating cost savings yourself
- Docs include token_efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) but do not give concrete dollar amounts or pricing.
- To compute monetary savings for your model, use:
  - old_cost = (tokens_before / 1000) * price_per_1k
  - new_cost = (tokens_after / 1000) * price_per_1k
  - cost_savings_pct = (old_cost − new_cost) / old_cost * 100
- Example with the documented example:
  - tokens_before = 500,000; tokens_after ≈ 5,000 → token_reduction_pct ≈ 99%
  - So you would pay roughly 1% of the original token cost (multiply by your model’s price_per_1k to get dollars).

If you want, tell me:
- your LLM’s price per 1k tokens (or provider/model), and whether you want session context included — I can compute a concrete cost example.

Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code lookups and rlm_decompose when the question is complex and should be split into sub‑questions you want to inspect or run separately.

Quick decision rules
- rlm_context_query — Single, focused documentation or code question (semantic search with token budgeting, shared context injected). Try this first for most Q&A and code lookups.
- rlm_decompose — Complex, multi‑facet tasks where you want to break the problem into subqueries, review/modify those subqueries, and then run them (often followed by rlm_multi_query to fetch each subtopic).

Examples / workflow
- Simple: "Where is auth handled?" → rlm_context_query.
- Complex: "Implement user authentication with email verification" → rlm_decompose to get subqueries (e.g., login flow, JWT handling, email service, verification flow), inspect/adjust them, then run rlm_multi_query or rlm_context_query per subquery.

Notes from docs
- rlm_decompose is explicitly for breaking complex queries and letting you inspect/modify subqueries.
- rlm_context_query is the primary full‑featured semantic search tool (shared context loaded first, token budgeting, etc.).

Use the mode that fits your intent — quick exact matches use keyword, conceptual/architectural questions use semantic, and anything needing both precision and relevance uses hybrid.

- Keyword — basic term matching. Use for:
  - Looking for a specific function or exact string
  - Error-code or log-line lookups
  - API endpoint discovery
  - Fast, cheap queries

- Semantic — embedding similarity (conceptual). Use for:
  - “How does X work?” or “How is auth implemented?”
  - Architecture understanding or design questions
  - Broad conceptual searches where wording varies

- Hybrid — combined keyword + semantic (best of both). Use for:
  - General documentation searches
  - Best-practices searches
  - When you want both precision and semantic relevance

Examples from the docs:
- Looking for specific function → keyword
- “How does X work?” → semantic
- General documentation search → hybrid
- Error code lookup → keyword
- Architecture understanding → semantic
- API endpoint discovery → keyword
- Best practices → hybrid

Note: Free-plan requests for semantic/hybrid will be downgraded to keyword; responses include search_mode_downgraded and a warning.
Not found in documentation.

Not found in documentation only applies if something is missing; the context does contain authentication info. Below are the authentication options described in the provided documentation and how they differ in security.

Available authentication options

1) API Key authentication
- How used: client sends X-API-Key: rlm_... header.
- Key format & storage: keys begin with the rlm_ prefix and are SHA-256 hashed in the database.
- Lookup behavior: server hashes the provided key and looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (project keys first, then team keys).
- Visibility: only the first 8 characters of the key are shown in UI/logs; logs use prefix-only for identification.
- Supported endpoints: MCP and Inject APIs accept X-API-Key; Inject API requires X-API-Key.
- Security-relevant notes from docs: hashed-at-rest in DB (SHA-256). The documentation does not state API-key expiry behavior or the exact revocation flow for API keys.

2) OAuth 2.0 (Device Flow) — Bearer token
- How used: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Token format & storage: access tokens have the snipara_at_ prefix; both access and refresh tokens are stored in the database as hashes. Token records include tokenPrefix (first 12 chars), scopes, clientName, accessExpiresAt and optional refreshExpiresAt.
- Device flow: supports RFC 8628-style device flow (CLI shows code, user authenticates in browser). Token polling endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token returns error authorization_pending while waiting and returns access_token / refresh_token on success.
- Validation & enforcement: DB lookup + expiry check, and scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") are enforced.
- Management: tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
- Security-relevant notes from docs: OAuth tokens have expirations and scopes and can be revoked; refresh tokens also tracked. The server checks expiry and will return HTTP 401 for invalid/expired OAuth tokens.

3) Local cached credentials / precedence behavior (operational auth behavior)
- The client/desktop stores OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json. The MCP server/client gives priority to a stored OAuth token (tokens.json) and falls back to API key from environment if no valid OAuth token is present.
- Because of caching, removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or moving it as a backup) and restarting Claude Code forces usage of API key authentication.
- Note: the MCP server process may cache credentials in memory; a full restart of the client/process is required to clear that cached state.

How they differ in security (summary)

- Scope control: OAuth tokens include scopes and thus support fine-grained, scope-limited access. API keys (as documented) do not show scope metadata in the same way.
- Expiration & refresh: OAuth access tokens have explicit expirations and refresh tokens; this limits window of exposure. The documentation does not describe expirations for API keys.
- Revocation & management: OAuth tokens can be listed and revoked via the API endpoints shown. API key revocation is not described in the provided snippets.
- Storage at rest: both API keys and OAuth tokens are stored hashed in the database (API keys with SHA-256; OAuth tokens are stored as hashes as well).
- Operational risk (client-side): OAuth tokens may be cached locally (tokens.json) and in-memory by processes; stale/invalid tokens can persist until revoked/removed and the process restarted. That caching behavior can make switching to API key auth require removing the tokens.json and restarting.
- Exposure model: API keys are a direct secret sent as X-API-Key; if an API key is leaked it can be used wherever that key is accepted. OAuth's short-lived access tokens and scope controls reduce risk compared with a long-lived secret (and allow revocation).

If you want specific steps for switching between these methods (for example, forcing API-key usage by removing cached OAuth tokens), the docs include the exact commands to remove/move ~/.snipara/tokens.json and note you must restart Claude Code.

Short answer
- The token budget is split between project docs and shared-context docs, and shared docs are further split by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
- Project docs are allocated first, then each shared category gets its category budget. Within each bucket, documents are sorted (relevanceScore if present) and included until that bucket’s token budget is exhausted; remaining docs are marked unallocated.

Details / step‑by‑step
- Allocation buckets:
  - Project (project-specific) is allocated first.
  - Shared categories are allocated next (separately): MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE.
- Default allocation (implementation): the code (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) defines DEFAULT_ALLOCATION:
  - project: 0.40 (40% of total budget)
  - MANDATORY: 0.25
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20
  - GUIDELINES: 0.10
  - REFERENCE: 0.05
  Note: higher‑level docs elsewhere show the conceptual shared-category split as 40%/30%/20%/10 for MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE; the actual allocation follows the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in the token-budget.ts implementation unless a customAllocation is provided.
- Allocation algorithm:
  1. Group docs by source and category.
  2. Compute budget per bucket (Math.floor(totalBudget * allocation[bucket])).
  3. For each bucket, sort docs by relevanceScore (if available) and include documents in order while tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount <= bucketBudget.
  4. Included docs are marked allocated=true with an allocationReason; others are marked allocated=false with reason "exceeded budget".
- Query ordering & params:
  - MCP/rlm_context_query typically loads shared context first (shared_context_budget_percent controls how much of the total budget is assigned to shared context — docs mention a default of 30% for that param), then uses the remaining budget for project docs.
  - rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query accept parameters to filter categories and control max_tokens/shared budget percent.
- Hard limits / safeguards:
  - LIMITS in code: MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT = 10, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100,000, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION = 50, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.2.
  - enforceCollectionLimits will truncate/omit documents from a collection if the collection’s total tokens exceed MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION (it keeps documents until the token cap is reached).
- Practical effect:
  - Shared context is guaranteed to be considered early and only important/shared-category docs (by budget + relevance) are included; project docs get a reserved portion (project bucket) and are always allocated before shared docs under the implemented allocation flow.
- Overrides:
  - The allocation mapping can be overridden via customAllocation passed to allocateTokenBudget, and query parameters (shared_context_budget_percent, categories, max_tokens) change how much shared context is requested/used.

If you want, I can show a short numeric example (e.g., totalBudget = 10,000) using the implemented DEFAULT_ALLOCATION.

RLM stands for Recursive Language Models.
The MCP server requires Python 3.10 or newer (Python 3.10+). The Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build with Python 3.12.

When the monthly query limit is exceeded the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with usage details. Example body:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Fields:
- current: current usage count this billing period
- max: maximum allowed queries (-1 = unlimited)
- exceeded: whether the limit has been reached (true)
- reset_at: ISO 8601 timestamp when the monthly limit resets

Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) are also included in responses.
Not found in documentation
